The 15 [counties](/wiki/County "County") are [administrative regions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") that are the first-level [administrative divisions of Norway](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Norway "Administrative divisions of Norway"). The counties are further subdivided into 357 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Norway "Municipalities of Norway") ([Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"): *kommune*). The island territories of [Svalbard](/wiki/Svalbard "Svalbard") and [Jan Mayen](/wiki/Jan_Mayen "Jan Mayen") are outside the county divisions and they are ruled directly from the national level. The capital city of [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo") is both a county and a municipality.

There are **15 counties in [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway").** The 15 [counties](/wiki/County "County") are [administrative regions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") that are the first-level [administrative divisions of Norway](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Norway "Administrative divisions of Norway"). The counties are further subdivided into 357 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Norway "Municipalities of Norway") ([Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"): *kommune*). The island territories of [Svalbard](/wiki/Svalbard "Svalbard") and [Jan Mayen](/wiki/Jan_Mayen "Jan Mayen") are outside the county divisions and they are ruled directly from the national level. The capital city of [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo") is both a county and a municipality.

In 2017, the [Solberg government](/wiki/Solberg%27s_Cabinet "Solberg's Cabinet") decided to abolish some of the counties and to merge them with other counties to form larger ones, reducing the number of counties from 19 to 11, which was implemented on 1 January 2020.[[1]](#cite_note-1) This sparked popular opposition, with some calling for the reform to be reversed. The Storting voted to partly undo the reform on 14 June 2022, with Norway to have 15 counties from 1 January 2024.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Three of the newly merged counties, namely [Vestfold og Telemark](/wiki/Vestfold_og_Telemark "Vestfold og Telemark"), [Viken](/wiki/Viken_(county) "Viken (county)")[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Roede-4) and [Troms og Finnmark](/wiki/Troms_og_Finnmark "Troms og Finnmark"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) were dissolved and the old counties existing before the reform re-established with a few minor changes as some municipalities merged across former county borders and some switched counties during the 2020 local government reform ([Regionreformen i Norge](/w/index.php?title=Regionreformen_i_Norge&action=edit&redlink=1 "Regionreformen i Norge (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regionreformen_i_Norge "no:Regionreformen i Norge")]).

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

The counties in Norway are called *fylke* (singular) and *fylker* (plural). This name comes from the [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") word *[fylki](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/fylki#Old_Norse "wikt:fylki")* which means "district" or "county", but it is similar to the same root as "folk". It is similar in the minority languages in Norway: [Northern Sami](/wiki/Northern_Sami_language "Northern Sami language"): *fylka*, [Southern Sami](/wiki/Southern_Sami_language "Southern Sami language"): *fylhke*, [Lule Sami](/wiki/Lule_Sami_language "Lule Sami language"): *fylkka*, [Kven](/wiki/Kven_language "Kven language"): *fylkki*. Prior to 1918, the counties were known as *[amt](/wiki/Amt_(administrative_division) "Amt (administrative division)")* (singular) or *amter* (plural).

## List of counties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: List of counties")]

Below is a list of the Norwegian counties, with their current administrative centres. The counties are administered both by appointees of the national government and by their own elected bodies. The county numbers are from the official numbering system [ISO 3166-2:NO](/wiki/ISO_3166-2:NO "ISO 3166-2:NO"), which originally was set up to follow the coastline from the Swedish border in the southeast to the Russian border in the northeast, but with the numbering has changed with county mergers.

The island territories of [Svalbard](/wiki/Svalbard "Svalbard") and [Jan Mayen](/wiki/Jan_Mayen "Jan Mayen") lie outside of the county system of Norway. Svalbard is administered by the [Governor of Svalbard](/wiki/Governor_of_Svalbard "Governor of Svalbard"), and Jan Mayen is administered by the [County Governor of Nordland](/wiki/List_of_County_Governors_of_Nordland "List of County Governors of Nordland") (but not part of Nordland).

## Responsibilities and significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Responsibilities and significance")]

Every county has two main organisations, both with underlying organisations.

1. The [county municipality](/wiki/County_municipality_(Norway) "County municipality (Norway)") ([Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"): *Fylkeskommune*) has a [county council](/wiki/County_council_(Norway) "County council (Norway)") ([Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"): *Fylkesting*), whose members are elected by the inhabitants. The county municipality is responsible mainly for some medium level schools, public transport organisation, regional road planning, culture, among other things.
2. The [county governor](/wiki/County_governor_(Norway) "County governor (Norway)") ([Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"): *Statsforvalteren*) is an authority directly overseen by the Norwegian government. It surveills the municipalities and receives complaints from people over their actions. It also controls areas where the government needs local direct ruling outside the municipalities.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

### *Fylke* (1st period)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Fylke (1st period)")]

From the consolidation to a single kingdom, [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") was divided into a number of geographic regions that each had its own legislative assembly or [Thing](/wiki/Thing_(assembly) "Thing (assembly)"), such as [Gulating](/wiki/Gulating "Gulating") ([Western Norway](/wiki/Western_Norway "Western Norway")) and [Frostating](/wiki/Frostating "Frostating") ([Trøndelag](/wiki/Tr%C3%B8ndelag "Trøndelag")). The second-order subdivision of these regions was into *fylker*, such as *[Egdafylke](/wiki/Agder "Agder")* and *[Hordafylke](/wiki/Hordaland "Hordaland")*. In 1914, the historical term *fylke* was brought into use again to replace the term [amt](/wiki/Amt_(administrative_division) "Amt (administrative division)") introduced during the [union](/wiki/Kalmar_union "Kalmar union") with [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark-Norway "Denmark-Norway"). Current day counties (fylker) often, but not necessarily, correspond to the historical areas.

### *Syssel*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Syssel")]

#### *Syssel* in 1300

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Syssel in 1300")]

### *Len*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Len")]

From 1308, the term *len* (plural *len*) in [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") signified an administrative region roughly equivalent to today's counties. The historic *len* was an important administrative entity during the period of [Dano-Norwegian](/wiki/Denmark-Norway "Denmark-Norway") unification after their [amalgamation](/wiki/Political_union "Political union") as one state, which lasted for the period 1536[[9]](#cite_note-9)–1814.

At the beginning of the 16th century the political divisions were variable, but consistently included four main *len* and approximately 30 smaller sub-regions with varying connections to a main *len*. Up to 1660 the four principal *len* were headquartered at the major fortresses [Bohus Fortress](/wiki/Bohus_Fortress "Bohus Fortress"), [Akershus Fortress](/wiki/Akershus_Fortress "Akershus Fortress"), [Bergenhus Fortress](/wiki/Bergenhus_Fortress "Bergenhus Fortress") and the fortified city of [Trondheim](/wiki/Trondheim_(city) "Trondheim (city)").[[10]](#cite_note-10) The sub-regions corresponded to the church districts for the [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") church in Norway.

#### *Len* in 1536

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Len in 1536")]

* [Båhus len](/wiki/B%C3%A5hus_len "Båhus len") (later termed [Bohuslän](/wiki/Bohusl%C3%A4n "Bohuslän") after Denmark-Norway ceded it to [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") by the [Treaty of Roskilde](/wiki/Treaty_of_Roskilde "Treaty of Roskilde") in 1658)
* [Akershus len](/w/index.php?title=Akershus_len&action=edit&redlink=1 "Akershus len (page does not exist)")
* [Trondheim len](/w/index.php?title=Trondheim_len&action=edit&redlink=1 "Trondheim len (page does not exist)")
* [Bergenhus len](/wiki/Bergenhus_len "Bergenhus len") (which included [Northern Norway](/wiki/Nord-Norge "Nord-Norge"))

These four principal *len* were in the 1530s divided into approximately 30 smaller regions. From that point forward through the beginning of the 17th century the number of subsidiary *len* was reduced, while the composition of the principal *len* became more stable.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### *Len* in 1660

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Len in 1660")]

From 1660 Norway had nine principal *len* comprising 17 subsidiary *len*:

* [Akershus len](/w/index.php?title=Akershus_len&action=edit&redlink=1 "Akershus len (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/Akershus_len "no:Akershus len")]
* [Tunsberg len](/w/index.php?title=Tunsberg_len&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tunsberg len (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%C3%B8nsberg_len "no:Tønsberg len")]
* [Bratsberg len](/w/index.php?title=Bratsberg_len&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bratsberg len (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bratsberg_len "no:Bratsberg len")]
* [Agdesiden len](/w/index.php?title=Agdesiden_len&action=edit&redlink=1 "Agdesiden len (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agdesiden_len "no:Agdesiden len")]
* [Stavanger len](/w/index.php?title=Stavanger_len&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stavanger len (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stavanger_len "no:Stavanger len")]
* [Bergenhus len](/wiki/Bergenhus_len "Bergenhus len")
* [Trondheim len](/w/index.php?title=Trondheim_len&action=edit&redlink=1 "Trondheim len (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trondhjems_len "no:Trondhjems len")]
* [Nordlandene len](/w/index.php?title=Nordlandene_len&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nordlandene len (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nordlandenes_len "no:Nordlandenes len")]
* [Vardøhus len](/w/index.php?title=Vard%C3%B8hus_len&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vardøhus len (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vard%C3%B8hus_len "no:Vardøhus len")]

*Len* written as *län* continues to be used as the administrative equivalent of county in Sweden to this day. Each *len* was governed by a *lenman*.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### *Amt*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Amt")]

With the royal decree of 19 February 1662, each *len* was designated an *amt* (plural *amt*) and the *lenmann* was titled *[amtmann](/wiki/Amtmann "Amtmann")*, from German *Amt* (office), reflecting the bias of the Danish court of that period.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### *Fylke* (2nd period)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Fylke (2nd period)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Norges_valgkretser.png/330px-Norges_valgkretser.png)](/wiki/File:Norges_valgkretser.png)

Counties of Norway between 1972 and 2018

From 1919 each *amt* was renamed a *fylke* (plural *fylke(r)*) (county) and the *amtmann* was now titled *fylkesmann* (county governor).

The county numbers are from the official numbering system [ISO 3166-2:NO](/wiki/ISO_3166-2:NO "ISO 3166-2:NO"), which originally was set up to follow the coastline from the Swedish border in the southeast to the Russian border in the northeast, but the numbering has changed with county mergers. The number 13, 16 and 17 were dropped, and the number 50 was added to account for changes over the years. The lack of a county number 13 is due to the city of [Bergen](/wiki/Bergen "Bergen") no longer being its own county, and is unrelated to [fear of the number 13](/wiki/Triskaidekaphobia "Triskaidekaphobia").

In 2018, [Sør-Trøndelag](/wiki/S%C3%B8r-Tr%C3%B8ndelag "Sør-Trøndelag") was merged with [Nord-Trøndelag](/wiki/Nord-Tr%C3%B8ndelag "Nord-Trøndelag") into the new county of [Trøndelag](/wiki/Tr%C3%B8ndelag "Trøndelag"), and several followed.

### *Fylke* (3rd period)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Counties_of_Norway&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Fylke (3rd period)")]

In 2017, the Norwegian government announced the merge of the existing 19 fylker into 11 new fylker by 2020. As a result, several government responsibilities were transferred to the new regions.[[13]](#cite_note-16)

New counties

* [Troms og Finnmark](/wiki/Troms_og_Finnmark "Troms og Finnmark"), by merging Finnmark and Troms counties in 2020. Disestablished in 2023, split into Finnmark and Troms counties.
* [Nordland](/wiki/Nordland "Nordland"), no change, same as Nordland county.
* [Trøndelag](/wiki/Tr%C3%B8ndelag "Trøndelag"), by merging Nord-Trøndelag and Sør-Trøndelag counties in 2018.
* [Møre og Romsdal](/wiki/M%C3%B8re_og_Romsdal "Møre og Romsdal"), no change, same as Møre og Romsdal county.
* [Vestland](/wiki/Vestland "Vestland"), by merging Hordaland and Sogn og Fjordane counties in 2020.
* [Rogaland](/wiki/Rogaland "Rogaland"), no change, same as Rogaland county.
* [Agder](/wiki/Agder "Agder"), by merging Aust-Agder and Vest-Agder counties in 2020.
* [Vestfold og Telemark](/wiki/Vestfold_og_Telemark "Vestfold og Telemark"), by merging Telemark and Vestfold counties in 2020. Disestablished in 2023, split into Telemark and Vestfold counties.
* [Innlandet](/wiki/Innlandet "Innlandet"), by merging Hedmark and Oppland counties in 2020.
* [Viken](/wiki/Viken_(Norwegian_county) "Viken (Norwegian county)"), by merging Akershus, Buskerud, and Østfold counties in 2020. Disestablished in 2023, split into Akershus, Buskerud, and Østfold counties.
* [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"), no change, same as Oslo county.
